referenced_message.content:
In general, container mode is convenient when you need to run a containerized application, so the best would be to ask ChatGPT how to create a Docker container for the app you want to run and upload it to DockerHub (or other container registry). Then you'll be able to run it with a single command `rift docker -x <instance> docker run <container> ...`

It is not very convenient to create an Ubuntu container and work in it. However, you can generally do that with:
```
rift docker -x <executor-id> run --rm -it ubuntu /bin/bash
```

It shows these instructions when you rent a node and choose "No Container" mode. You need to be aware of port forwarding and other Docker-specific things, though, so I generally don't recommend it for tinkering.

We're working with new providers on adding more RTX 4090 and RTX 5090 supply.

content:
In general, container mode is convenient when you need to run a containerized application, so the best would be to ask ChatGPT how to create a Docker container for the app you want to run and upload it to DockerHub (or other container registry). It is not very convenient to create an Ubuntu container and work in it. However, you can generally do that with:
```
rift docker -x <executor-id> run --rm -it ubuntu /bin/bash
```

It shows these instructions when you rent a node and choose "No Container" mode. You need to be aware of port forwarding and other Docker-specific things, though, so I generally don't recommend it for tinkering.

If you don't stop the machine (don't press the stop button) - all your containers should be intact and you can connect to the one using:

fair docker ps

content:
If you don't stop the machine (don't press the stop button) - all your containers should be intact and you can connect to the one using:
```
fair docker ps
fair docker exec -it bash <container_name>
```
However, you're charged as long as you have a running machine.

If you stop the machine, then all your containers are removed. This is done because other users might need the resources that you were using.

In the future we will implement the feature that allows you save the state of your machine, but for now this is the limitation of the platform.

One workaround that you can use for now is to create your own Docker image with all the files you need. Then you can start the machine based of your own image and have everything you need: https://depot.dev/blog/docker-build-image

Note, though, that the `--rm` argument will remove the container once you've finished working with it, so if you want it running, don't use it.

Use the `fair docker ps` command to see what is running on your cluster.

Use `fair docker kill` to kill the containers you no longer need.

Thanks for the feedback. The installer is not installing system dependencies. They differ depending on Linux distribution, so we didn't bother incorporating them into the installer script.

Here is the list of dependencies we're using for Linux development:
`sudo apt-get install -y clang lld libssl-dev libgtk-3-dev libayatana-appindicator3-dev libxdo-dev`

I prefer installing fair as a system service on Linux. It is more lightweight, doesn't have GUI dependencies and standard system service utilities can be used to manage it. It can be installed using:
`curl https://faircompute.com/install-fair-service.sh | sudo sh`

And then
`sudo fair configure`
